children.name: Asterias
children.description: A genus of starfish from northern waters, typically with five arms and a rough surface covered in small spines. Asterias rubens is one of the most abundant echinoderms of the North Atlantic and a keystone predator of mussel beds.

children.name: Asterias rubens
children.description: The common starfish, common sea star or sugar starfish is the most common and familiar starfish in the north-east Atlantic. Belonging to the family Asteriidae, it has five arms and usually grows to between 10–30 cm across, although larger specimens are known. The common starfish is usually orange or brownish in color, and sometimes violet; specimens found in deeper waters are pale.

children.name: Asterias forbesi
children.description: Asterias forbesi, commonly known as Forbes sea star, is a species of starfish in the family Asteriidae. It is found in shallow waters in the northwest Atlantic Ocean and the Caribbean Sea.

children.name: Asterias amurensis
children.description: Asterias amurensis, also known as the Northern Pacific seastar and Japanese common starfish, is a seastar found in shallow seas and estuaries, native to the coasts of northern China, Korea, far eastern Russia, Japan, Alaska, the Aleutian Islands and British Columbia in Canada. Two forms are recognised: the nominate and forma robusta from the Strait of Tartary. It mostly preys on large bivalve molluscs, and it is mostly preyed on by other species of starfish.

children.name: Pisaster
children.description: A genus of large starfish from the Pacific coast of North America, known for their ecological role as keystone predators in intertidal communities.

children.name: Pisaster ochraceus
children.description: Pisaster ochraceus, generally known as the purple sea star, ochre sea star, or ochre starfish, is a common seastar found among the waters of the Pacific Ocean. Identified as a keystone species, P. ochraceus is considered an important indicator for the health of the intertidal zone.

children.name: Pisaster giganteus
children.description: Pisaster giganteus, the giant sea star, giant spined star, or knobby sea star is a species of sea star that lives along the western coast of North America from Southern California to British Columbia. It makes its home on rocky shores near the low tide mark. It preys on mollusks.

children.name: Leptasterias
children.description: A genus of small six-rayed starfish found in cold northern waters. Species are often brooding, retaining developing young in the oral cavity.

children.name: Leptasterias muelleri
children.description: Leptasterias muelleri, the northern starfish, is a species of starfish in the family Asteriidae. It is found in the Arctic Ocean and the northern Atlantic Ocean. It is a predator and scavenger and is unusual among starfish in that it broods its young.

children.name: Leptasterias polaris
children.description: Leptasterias polaris, the polar six-rayed star, is a species of starfish in the family Asteriidae. It is found in cold waters in the northwest Atlantic Ocean and in polar regions.

children.name: Henricia
children.description: A genus of brightly coloured starfish with a smooth, fine-textured surface and typically five long slender arms. Feed on sponges and bryozoans by filtering suspended particles.

children.name: Henricia oculata
children.description: Henricia oculata, commonly known as the bloody Henry starfish, is a species of starfish in the family Echinasteridae. It is native to northwestern Europe. It was first described as Asterias oculata by the British zoologist Thomas Pennant in 1777, later being transferred to the genus Henricia.

children.name: Henricia sanguinolenta
children.description: Henricia sanguinolenta, commonly known as the northern henricia, is a species of sea star from the North Atlantic and North Pacific Oceans. Henricia sanguinolenta is very similar to Henricia oculata, also known as "bloody Henry", and the two can only be distinguished by laboratory tests. It comes in colors of red, yellow, orange, purple, and lavender.

children.name: Coscinasterias
children.description: A genus of large, multi-armed starfish found in temperate waters worldwide. Known for asexual reproduction through fission.

children.name: Coscinasterias tenuispina
children.description: A Mediterranean and eastern Atlantic starfish with up to eleven arms, distinguished by blue spots on a grey or brown body. Reproduces by splitting into two individuals.

children.name: Coscinasterias muricata
children.description: Coscinasterias muricata is a species of starfish in the family Asteriidae. It is a large 11-armed starfish and occurs in shallow waters in the temperate western Indo-Pacific region.
